title: Myth, Media, and the Public Image of The Beatles
content: The Beatles have become so surrounded by myth, fantasy and speculation that determining anything other than the basic facts of their lives has become virtually impossible.

The way the public perceives the really famous is an amalgamation of information filtered through layers of the media, embellished, stereotyped, politicised according to the position of the writers and editors involved.

title: Why Reliable Beatles Interviews Are Rare
content: In the case of rock stars, particularly unreliable since the media – TV shows and the popular music press – are more a port of the entertainment business than serious journalism of record, a situation exacerbated by musicians, who usually only give interviews to promote new product.

There was virtually no serious coverage given to pop music by the press until the latter half of the sixties, when the underground press – East Village Other, Berkely Barb, IT – started up, and at the end of the decade, when magazines like Rolling Stone began publication. As a consequence there are very few reliable interviews with the Beatles at the height of their fame.

title: The Beatles as Cultural Iconography of the 1960s
content: Received knowledge is a strange mélange of the Fab Four, the four mop tops, the Lords of Psychedelia, the witty one, the cute one and, in the case of John, the Prince of Peace, combining the wisecracking happy chappies of A Hard Day's Night and the tripped-out figures of the 'Strawberry Fields Forever' promotional film.
The chirpy cartoon cut-outs of Yellow Submarine and the hallucinatory Richard Avedon portrait posters which adorned so many bedroom walls.
The stress was moreover always on the Beatles as a group, not as individuals. They became a part of popular iconography, a convenient signifier of sixties Britain.
Just as the Eiffel Tower symbolizes Paris, a few frames of Harold Wilson, Christine Keeler and the Beatles now define an entire era.
